From the code:
We are assuming that the function definitions have no early-bound parameters here, but in Rust they do, how should we handle this? e.g., consider
fn foo<'a, T>(x: &'a T) { }
in Rust this yields a type like `for<'a> foo<'a, ?X>`.

These are not modeled at all and they are confusing AF.
I don't have concrete idea on how to do that yet, feel free to add more description.

In minirust, if this is value None, then UB will be raised when the function returns. I personally think we can make just make this value not an ``Option``, so our MIR that passed parsing is always valid.

#248: Cleanup: nicer pattern for Arc in

a.xlink:title:
Follow-up cleanup from PR #246:
`nll.rs` has a clone-heavy pattern for building an immutable stack:
let stack = { let mut s = stack.clone(); s.push(this_entry.clone()); s }
This could be made more readable, possibly with a helper or a different data structure.
See: https://github.com/rust-lang/a-mir-formality/pull/246#discussion_r2889222269

Follow-up cleanup from PR #246:
`TypedPlaceExpression` struct literal construction in `nll.rs` requires explicit `Arc::new` and `.clone()` calls:
root: Arc::new(root_expr.clone()), ty: ty.clone(), kind: TypedPlaceExpressionKind::Deref(Arc::new(inner_expr.clone())),…

Summary
The left-recursion fixed-point loop selects only the longest base-case parse as the seed for each iteration. When a shorter base-case parse would compose with a left-recursive variant to produce a valid complete parse, that composition is never explored.
Reproducing grammar
E = E . then . end // left-recursive: needs ". then . end" after E | $id .…
https://github.com/rust-lang/a-mir-formality/pull/244 expands the borrowck implementation for what we want to reach in the future: more precision, less errors, etc.…

As discussed in #t-types/formality on Zulip, it would be helpful to expand the a-mir-formality to make it easier for new contributors to get started. Currently the Intro page is empty and the README lists crate names that no longer match the workspace.
1.…

The proof rules output says "evaluted" instead of "evaluated" in several places.
The source of the typo is here in `proven_set.rs`. Fix it there, then run:
UPDATE_EXPECT=1 cargo test
to update the expect-test snapshots automatically.
condition evaluted to false: `place_disjoint_from_place(...)`
should be:
condition evaluated to false: `place_disjoint_from_place(...)`

Tracking issue for ensuring we have both borrow-checker (`borrowck.rs`) and type-checking (`mir_typeck.rs`) test coverage for every `Expr`, `Stmt`, and `PlaceExpr` variant.
Current coverage gaps…

Parent tracking issue: #265
The `ExprData::False` variant has no test coverage in either `borrowck.rs` or `mir_typeck.rs`.
Add at least one test exercising the `false` literal, e.g.: - A borrow-check test with `if false { ... } else { ... }` - A type-check test using `false` as an expression
See the parent tracking issue for instructions on how to add tests and which files to edit.

Parent tracking issue: #265
The `Stmt::If` variant has borrow-check coverage (e.g., `min_problem_case_3`) but no type-checking coverage in `mir_typeck.rs`.
Add a test that exercises an `if` statement in the surface language. For example, a function that branches on a condition and returns different values from each branch.…

Parent tracking issue: #265
The `ExprData::True` variant has borrow-check coverage (e.g., `min_problem_case_3`) but no type-checking coverage in `mir_typeck.rs`.
Add a test that uses the `true` literal. This could be combined with a `Stmt::If` test. See the parent tracking issue for instructions on how to add tests.

Parent tracking issue: #265
The `PlaceExprData::Parens` variant has borrow-check coverage (e.g., `problem_case_4`) but no type-checking coverage in `mir_typeck.rs`.
Add a test that uses a parenthesized place expression, e.g., `(*v).field` where the deref needs parens to parse correctly. See the parent tracking issue for instructions on how to add tests.

Parent tracking issue: #265
The `ExprData::Call` variant has type-checking coverage (e.g., `test_call_terminator`) but no borrow-check coverage in `borrowck.rs`.
Add borrow-check tests exercising function calls, e.g.: - Passing a borrowed reference to a function - Calling a function while a borrow is live - A function that takes `&mut` preventing aliased access…

Parent tracking issue: #265
The `ExprData::Turbofish` variant has type-checking coverage (e.g., `test_call_generic_fn_with_turbofish`) but no borrow-check coverage in `borrowck.rs`.
Add a borrow-check test that calls a generic function with explicit type/lifetime parameters using turbofish syntax (e.g., `identity::<u32>(v)`).
See the parent tracking issue for instructions on how to add tests.

Parent tracking issue: #265
The `ExprData::Struct` variant has type-checking coverage (e.g., `test_struct`) but no borrow-check coverage in `borrowck.rs`.
Add borrow-check tests exercising struct construction, e.g.: - Constructing a struct that borrows from a local - Accessing a struct field while another field is borrowed
See the parent tracking issue for instructions on how to add tests.

Tracking issue for converting the top-level checking code from regular Rust functions (that manually construct proof trees) into judgment functions.
How it works today
a-mir-formality begins today by calling `check_all_crates`.…

Parent tracking issue: #288
Convert `check_trait` from a regular Rust function to a judgment function.
Currently it: opens the trait binder, proves where-clauses are well-formed, and checks each trait item.…

Parent tracking issue: #288
Convert `check_trait_impl` from a regular Rust function to a judgment function.
Currently it: opens the binder, proves WF + is_implemented + not_implemented, checks safety, and checks impl items.…

Parent tracking issue: #288
Convert `check_neg_trait_impl` from a regular Rust function to a judgment function.
Currently it: opens the binder, proves WF + not_implemented, and validates that negative impls are not declared unsafe.
See the parent tracking issue for context and examples.
This issue has been assigned to @JojoFlex1 via this comment.

Parent tracking issue: #288
Convert `check_adt` from a regular Rust function to a judgment function.
Currently it: opens the ADT binder, proves where-clauses are well-formed, and proves each field type is well-formed.
See the parent tracking issue for context and examples.

Parent tracking issue: #288
Convert `check_free_fn` and `check_fn` from regular Rust functions to judgment functions.
`check_free_fn` is a thin wrapper that calls `check_fn` with an empty env.
`check_fn` currently: proves WF of where-clauses + argument types + return type, then dispatches to the borrow checker for the function body.…

Parent tracking issue: #288
Convert `check_coherence` from a regular Rust function to a judgment function.
Currently it orchestrates: - `orphan_check` / `orphan_check_neg` — proves is_local - `overlap_check` — proves non-overlap via not_goal or inverted where-clauses
These helpers should also be converted.
See the parent tracking issue for context and examples.

Updates #254. Related to #209.
The borrow checker currently does not track whether variables are initialized or have been moved. This means programs that use uninitialized variables or use variables after they've been moved are accepted when they should be rejected.
For example, this should be an error but currently passes:…

You cannot move a value out from behind a reference (`&T` or `&mut T`). Currently formality allows this.
In rustc this is error E0507: "cannot move out of `*r` which is behind a shared/mutable reference." This is a property of the place being behind a reference, not about active loans — it applies even if there are no other borrows.…

You cannot move a place that currently has an active loan. Currently formality detects conflicts when writing to a borrowed place, but does not specifically check moves.
In rustc this is error E0505: "cannot move out of `x` because it is borrowed."…

PR #282 left an odd error in `src/test/borrowck.rs`. See the conversation here:
https://github.com/rust-lang/a-mir-formality/pull/282#discussion_r3019048262
Currently, our test is in both ``src/test`` and ``tests``, it will be nice to have only one place for all tests.

From #310, I think we should add a new form into the judgment-fn macro like `(fail "...", ...)`. This would create an anyhow error that fails. It would be used to document cases that are intentionally omitted and to produce better error messages (e.g., moving from a `*` is not accepted).

Currently we have Arc-wrapper types in a number of places--
https://github.com/rust-lang/a-mir-formality/blob/0e9b18c8bcf5cab4f9e30d132c1d7d6cf2dcc890/crates/formality-rust/src/grammar/ty.rs#L14-L19
and an inner enum type
https://github.com/rust-lang/a-mir-formality/blob/0e9b18c8bcf5cab4f9e30d132c1d7d6cf2dcc890/crates/formality-rust/src/grammar/ty.rs#L118-L130
---which I hate.…
As noted in <https://github.com/rust-lang/a-mir-formality/pull/323#discussion_r3080736286>, we are starting to need to model the actual `Copy` trait in borrow check.

Description
The `PlaceExpr` grammar is currently ambiguous when combining dereferences and field projections. For example, the expression `*p.f` causes a parser panic because the formality core cannot distinguish between `*(p.f)` and `(*p).f` This was identified during the implementation of borrow-check coverage in PR #282.
Expected Outcome…

Summary
Refactor the test infrastructure to use a builder-style `FormalityTest` API with method chaining, replacing the current `assert_ok!` / `assert_err!` / `assert_rustc_success!` / `assert_rustc_error!` macros.
Motivation
Today we have separate macros for each kind of assertion (`assert_ok!`, `assert_err!`, `assert_rustc_success!`, `assert_rustc_error!`, `assert_equivalence!`).…
